strands.title: Task Execution & Polling Pattern
strands.content: Three sequential bash task submissions (bnk191til, b583n243e, and prior b5mvz85yy) remain in 'running' state across three consecutive polling cycles. Each task returns 'not_ready' status with identical timeout patterns. This suggests either: (a) long-running background operations that legitimately require extended wait time; (b) a resource contention pattern; (c) a polling loop that may need termination criteria.
key_claims: Task bnk191til is in running state after at least 2 polling cycles
Task b583n243e entered at cycle 197 with 5000ms timeout, still running at cycle 198
Polling frequency appears to be per-cycle with no exponential backoff visible

strands.title: Unresolved Task Semantics
strands.content: The actual purpose, input, and expected output of these tasks remain undeclared. Task IDs are opaque identifiers. No user-facing goal is visible that these tasks serve. Previous cycle identified this as critical gap — still unresolved.

strands.title: Resource & Timeout Management Risk
strands.content: No explicit timeout exceeded yet, but polling is now at cycle 66 with at least 3 distinct task IDs across 6 turns. 5000ms timeout on b583n243e (cycle 197) means potential hard stop at cycle 197+timeout window. Need to establish: (1) maximum acceptable polling depth; (2) fallback/retry criteria; (3) whether tasks are independent or dependent.
key_claims: Timeout window established for b583n243e at 5000ms
No max polling depth currently specified

declared_losses.detail: Why these specific tasks (b5mvz85yy, bnk191til, b583n243e) were initiated remains unrecorded. No user directive, no semantic anchor to session goals. This context was likely in earlier conversation but is not present in current view.
declared_losses.impact: Prevents semantic grounding of polling strategy. Cannot assess whether continued polling is correct behavior.

declared_losses.detail: Wall-clock time between cycles is unknown. Cannot distinguish between 'tasks running slowly' vs. 'we're polling too aggressively'. Cycle count is abstract.
declared_losses.impact: Impossible to assess timeout risk or recommend polling interval adjustments.

declared_losses.detail: Previous tensor indicated b5mvz85yy was under observation. Cycle 66 shows no new polling of b5mvz85yy — was it abandoned, did it complete, did it fail? Unknown.
declared_losses.impact: Incomplete narrative of task lifecycle. Cannot inform next decisions about b583n243e.

open_questions: At what cycle/wall-clock time will b583n243e timeout? Is that timeout hard or soft?
Should polling continue indefinitely, or is there a max polling depth threshold?
Are these tasks (b5mvz85yy, bnk191til, b583n243e) independent, or does task N depend on task N-1's output?
What is the user-facing goal these tasks serve? Why were they initiated?
Has b5mvz85yy been abandoned? What was its final state?
Should the system implement exponential backoff or request batching to reduce polling frequency?
If all tasks timeout, what is the fallback behavior?
instructions_for_next: Next cycle (67) must: (1) Poll both bnk191til and b583n243e for status update — if either transitions to 'ready' or error state, integrate result into semantic strands immediately; (2) Check whether b583n243e timeout window (5000ms from cycle 197) has passed — if so, declare timeout and trigger fallback; (3) Establish polling depth limit (suggest max 5-10 more cycles before escalation); (4) If both remain 'running', prepare PAUSE recommendation with rationale: either (a) tasks are legitimately long-running (acceptable), or (b) system is stuck in polling loop (requires intervention); (5) Re-surface the unresolved question of task PURPOSE — cannot continue blind polling without understanding what these tasks are for; (6) Maintain strict accounting of which task IDs are active vs. abandoned.
